title: Able Care | Falls Prevention Technology
description: Digital, objective falls risk screening for home care, senior living and clinical teams. Four validated metrics in under five minutes. Evidence-based. Standardised. Built for scale.

blocks.headline: Keep people strong, steady and independent for life.

blocks.heading: Four metrics. One complete picture of function.

metrics.name: Grip strength
metrics.whatItMeasures: Upper-body strength proxy captured by the GripAble sensor.
metrics.whyItMatters: Predicts all-cause mortality more reliably than systolic blood pressure. A validated marker of frailty, sarcopenia and falls risk.

metrics.name: Sit-to-stand
metrics.whatItMeasures: Lower-limb power and balance. Timed five-repetition protocol.
metrics.whyItMatters: Independently predicts falls in older adults. Captures functional decline that grip strength alone can miss.

metrics.name: Gait speed
metrics.whatItMeasures: Walking speed over a standardised distance.
metrics.whyItMatters: Known as the 'sixth vital sign' for older adults. A strong predictor of hospitalisation, disability and mortality.

metrics.name: Timed Up and Go
metrics.whatItMeasures: Time to rise from a chair, walk three metres, turn, walk back and sit down.
metrics.whyItMatters: The most widely used clinical falls risk screen. Captures mobility, balance and transfer ability in a single measure.

steps.title: Capture
steps.description: Run the five-minute Able Assess workflow on any tablet. No clinical training required.

steps.title: Score
steps.description: Instant risk banding against age- and sex-matched normative data.

steps.title: Act
steps.description: Care plan triggers and alerts routed to the right team automatically.

steps.title: Track
steps.description: Longitudinal trends surface decline early, so you can intervene before a fall.

testimonials.quote: We caught decline six weeks earlier than we would have with our old process.
It paid for itself in the first quarter.

blocks.heading: Backed by the research
blocks.pulledStat: Grip strength predicts all-cause mortality more reliably than systolic blood pressure.

citations.title: Prognostic value of grip strength: findings from the Prospective Urban Rural Epidemiology (PURE) study
citations.finding: Grip strength was a stronger predictor of all-cause and cardiovascular mortality than systolic blood pressure across 17 countries.

citations.title: Multifactorial falls risk screening outperforms single-test approaches
citations.finding: Multifactorial assessment combining strength, balance and gait measures identified significantly more at-risk older adults than any single screening tool.

citations.title: Objective screening reduces falls incidence by 25%
citations.finding: Standardised, objective falls risk screening programmes reduced falls incidence by approximately 25% in community-dwelling older adults.

citations.title: Grip Strength: An Indispensable Biomarker For Older Adults
citations.finding: Grip strength is an indispensable biomarker for predicting outcomes in older adults across multiple health domains.

citations.title: Hand grip strength as a proposed new vital sign of health
citations.journal: Journal of Health and Population Nutrition
citations.finding: Grip strength proposed as a new vital sign of health, with evidence supporting its role across the lifespan.

citations.title: Functionally impaired patients face up to 42% higher readmission risk
citations.finding: Functional impairment at discharge was independently associated with a 42% increased risk of 30-day hospital readmission.

caseStudies.title: Home care: earlier intervention, fewer hospitalisations
caseStudies.summary: A PE-backed home care operator rolled out Able Assess across 12 branches. Non-clinical staff ran screenings in under five minutes, flagging at-risk clients six weeks earlier than their previous paper-based process.

caseStudies.title: Senior living: protecting occupancy through prevention
caseStudies.metricLabel: fewer falls per resident per year
caseStudies.summary: A CCRC with 200 IL and AL units adopted Able Assess as part of their quarterly wellness programme. Resident engagement increased and fall-related move-outs dropped.

caseStudies.title: Clinical research: objective endpoints for decentralised trials
caseStudies.metricLabel: validated functional endpoints in one workflow
caseStudies.summary: A CRO-led GLP-1 trial used Able Assess to capture grip strength and functional health remotely, replacing site-based Jamar assessments with digital, standardised capture.

blocks.heading: Model the financial impact of standardised falls screening.
blocks.body: Segment-aware. Two minutes. Emailable report. See how much you could save by reducing one fall per branch per month.

faqs.question: What hardware does Able Assess require?
faqs.answer: Able Assess runs on any iOS or Android tablet paired with the GripAble sensor for grip strength measurement. No specialist equipment beyond the sensor is needed.

faqs.question: How much training do staff need?
faqs.answer: Minimal. Able Assess was built for non-clinical staff. Most teams are running assessments confidently within a single onboarding session. No clinical qualification is required.

faqs.question: Does Able Assess integrate with our EHR or care management system?
faqs.answer: Yes. Able Assess is integration-ready with EHR, OASIS and wellness platforms. The clinical team can walk you through the integration during a demo.

faqs.question: Where is patient data stored?
faqs.answer: Able Care is ISO 27001 certified, HIPAA aligned and GDPR compliant. Data is encrypted at rest and in transit. Residency options are available for US and UK deployments.

faqs.question: How is Able Assess different from paper-based assessments like the Morse Fall Scale?
faqs.answer: Paper assessments are subjective, slow and difficult to standardise across staff. Able Assess captures four objective, peer-reviewed metrics in under five minutes, scores automatically against normative data, and routes results to the care team. No subjective scoring. No inter-rater variability.

blocks.heading: See Able Assess in your workflow.
blocks.bodyText: A 20-minute demo with a clinician who has run it in the field.
